content.text: Directed Acyclic Graphs and Task Dependencies in Workflow Orchestration

Workflow orchestration is the science of running the right tasks, in the right order, at the right time вҖ” and DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) are the mathematical foundation that makes this possible. Instead of writing scripts that call functions sequentially, a DAG lets you declare
, and the orchestrator figures out the optimal execution order, handles failures, retries, and scales across machines.
is a graph composed of nodes and directed edges with one critical constraint: no cycles. In workflow orchestration:
represent tasks (extract data, run a model, send a report)
represent dependencies ("task B must run after task A finishes")
means there is no path from any node back to itself вҖ” no circular dependencies

This structure encodes the complete dependency graph of a pipeline. The orchestrator reads this graph and determines which tasks can run in parallel, which must wait, and what order guarantees that all dependencies are satisfied before a task begins.

The term DAG comes from graph theory, but in the data engineering world it has become synonymous with a pipeline definition вҖ” whether you're using Apache Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, Luigi, or Metaflow.
The naive approach to data pipelines is a single Python script that runs steps one by one:
This breaks down immediately in production because:
fails, you re-run everything from the start, wasting time and money
Steps that could run in parallel are forced to run sequentially
There is no visibility into which step failed or why
Retrying a single step is manual and error-prone
Scheduling, monitoring, and alerting require separate solutions stitched together
By modeling a pipeline as a DAG, orchestration platforms can:
вҖ” if task C and task D both only depend on task A, they can run simultaneously
showing the current state of every task
of every run, making debugging and auditing straightforward
Think of building a house. You cannot paint the walls before they are built. You cannot build the walls before the foundation is poured. But you
lay the plumbing and electrical wiring at the same time, since neither depends on the other вҖ” they only both depend on the foundation.

A DAG is the blueprint that captures these relationships. The construction crew (orchestrator) reads the blueprint and coordinates workers (compute resources) to do as much in parallel as possible while respecting every dependency constraint.

The "acyclic" constraint has a practical meaning: a task cannot depend on itself or on a downstream task. You cannot paint the walls and then use the painted walls as a dependency to build the walls вҖ” that would be circular and logically impossible to resolve.

A в”Җв”Җв–ә B в”Җв”Җв–ә D
в”Ӯ         в–І
в””в”Җв”Җв–ә C в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
A has no dependencies вҖ” it runs first
B depends on A вҖ” runs after A completes
C depends on A вҖ” runs after A completes (in parallel with B)
D depends on both B and C вҖ” runs only when both finish
The orchestrator resolves this by computing a

вҖ” a linear ordering of nodes such that for every directed edge (u вҶ’ v), node u comes before node v. Multiple valid topological sorts may exist, and the orchestrator picks one (or runs parallel branches simultaneously).
The standard algorithm (Kahn's algorithm) works as follows:
(number of incoming edges) for every node
Add all nodes with in-degree 0 to a queue (these have no dependencies)
Pop a node from the queue, "execute" it, and remove its outgoing edges
Decrement the in-degree of all its neighbors; add any that reach in-degree 0 to the queue
Repeat until the queue is empty
If at the end not all nodes were processed, a cycle exists вҖ” the graph is not a valid DAG.
Architecture вҖ” How Orchestrators Model DAGs
Modern orchestrators follow a common architecture even though their APIs differ.

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                  DAG Definition                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (Python file / YAML / decorator-based code)         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                        в”Ӯ
                        в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                   Scheduler                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Parses DAG, checks schedule triggers, creates       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  DAG Runs, resolves dependency order                 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                        в”Ӯ submits tasks
                        в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                   Executor / Worker Pool             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Runs individual task instances in processes,        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  containers, or remote compute                       в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                        в”Ӯ reports status
                        в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                   Metadata Store                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Database recording task states, run history,        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  logs, XComs (inter-task data passing)               в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
The scheduler continuously polls the metadata store, checks which tasks have all dependencies in a
state, and submits them to the executor. The executor runs each task and writes the result (success, failure, retry) back to the metadata store.
Every task instance in a DAG run progresses through a state machine:

NONE в”Җв”Җв–ә SCHEDULED в”Җв”Җв–ә QUEUED в”Җв”Җв–ә RUNNING в”Җв”Җв–ә SUCCESS
                                     в”Ӯ
                                     в”ңв”Җв”Җв–ә FAILED в”Җв”Җв–ә (retry?) в”Җв”Җв–ә UP_FOR_RETRY
                                     в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ
                                     в””в”Җв”Җв–ә SKIPPED         в”Җв”Җв–ә FAILED (max retries)
state until all their upstream dependencies reach
. This state machine is managed by the metadata store and checked by the scheduler on every tick.
Implementing DAGs вҖ” Three Major Orchestrators
Airflow is the most widely adopted open-source orchestrator. A DAG is defined as a Python file placed in a

from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator

# Task functions
def extract(**context):
    data = {"rows": 1000, "source": "api"}
    # Push to XCom so downstream tasks can access it
    context["ti"].xcom_push(key="raw_data", value=data)
    print("Extracted data")

def transform(**context):
    raw = context["ti"].xcom_pull(task_ids="extract_task", key="raw_data")
    transformed = {**raw, "cleaned": True}
    context["ti"].xcom_push(key="clean_data", value=transformed)
    print(f"Transformed: {transformed}")

def load(**context):
    data = context["ti"].xcom_pull(task_ids="transform_task", key="clean_data")
    print(f"Loading to warehouse: {data}")

# DAG definition
default_args = {
    "owner": "data-team",
    "retries": 3,
    "retry_delay": timedelta(minutes=5),
    "email_on_failure": True,
}

with DAG(
    dag_id="etl_pipeline",
    default_args=default_args,
    schedule_interval="@daily",          # runs every day at midnight UTC
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,                       # don't backfill missed runs
    tags=["etl", "production"],
) as dag:

    extract_task = PythonOperator(
        task_id="extract_task",
        python_callable=extract,
    )

    transform_task = PythonOperator(
        task_id="transform_task",
        python_callable=transform,
    )

    load_task = PythonOperator(
        task_id="load_task",
        python_callable=load,
    )

    # Define dependencies using >> (bit-shift operator)
    extract_task >> transform_task >> load_task

# extract runs first, then clean and validate run in parallel,
# then load waits for both
extract_task >> [clean_task, validate_task] >> load_task
Prefect uses a decorator-based approach where a regular Python function becomes a
. Dependencies are inferred from Python data flow вҖ” if task B receives the return value of task A as an argument, Prefect knows B depends on A.
and do not depend on each other, Prefect runs them in parallel automatically using a task runner (e.g.,

from prefect import flow, task
from prefect.futures import wait

@flow
def parallel_pipeline():
    futures = [process_chunk.submit(chunk_id=i) for i in range(10)]
    results = [f.result() for f in futures]
    aggregate(results)
Dagster centres on the concept of
вҖ” instead of defining tasks, you define the data assets they produce. Dependencies between assets automatically create the DAG.
Dagster automatically builds the DAG from the function argument names matching asset names. The resulting dependency graph is:

raw_data в”Җв”Җв–ә cleaned_data в”Җв”Җв–ә warehouse_load
         в””в”Җв”Җв–ә validated_data в”Җв”ҳ
Not all dependencies are "wait for success." Modern orchestrators support nuanced dependency types:

from airflow.utils.trigger_rule import TriggerRule

# This cleanup task runs if ANY upstream task fails
cleanup_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id="cleanup_on_failure",
    python_callable=cleanup,
    trigger_rule=TriggerRule.ONE_FAILED,
)

# This notification task runs regardless of upstream outcome
notify_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id="send_notification",
    python_callable=notify,
    trigger_rule=TriggerRule.ALL_DONE,
)
Dynamic DAGs and Dynamic Task Mapping
Static DAGs have a fixed number of tasks known at definition time.
creates tasks at runtime based on data.
task instances that all run in parallel, then feeds their results as a list to
Inter-Task Communication вҖ” Passing Data Between Tasks
Tasks in a DAG are isolated processes. There are two strategies for sharing data:
Orchestrators provide a key-value store for small data exchange between tasks. Airflow calls this
XCom data is stored in the metadata database вҖ”
use it only for small metadata
(IDs, counts, file paths, status flags). Do not push large DataFrames through XCom.
For large datasets, tasks should write to shared external storage and pass only the reference:

# Producer writes to S3, passes the path
def extract(**context):
    df = fetch_from_api()
    path = f"s3://my-bucket/runs/{context['run_id']}/raw.parquet"
    df.to_parquet(path)
    context["ti"].xcom_push(key="data_path", value=path)

# Consumer reads from S3 using the path
def transform(**context):
    path = context["ti"].xcom_pull(task_ids="extract", key="data_path")
    df = pd.read_parquet(path)
    # ... transform ...
This pattern decouples task execution from data movement and allows arbitrarily large datasets.
The pipeline has multiple steps with real dependency relationships
Steps can fail independently and need isolated retry logic
The pipeline runs on a schedule (daily, hourly, event-triggered)
You need auditability вҖ” a record of every run and its outcome
Tasks should run in parallel where possible for speed
Multiple teams share and depend on the same pipelines
The pipeline will evolve over time and needs maintainability
Do not use a full DAG orchestrator when:
You have a single sequential script that runs in seconds
The pipeline is purely exploratory or a one-time migration
Operational overhead of running an orchestrator outweighs the benefits (for small teams / simple jobs, a cron job + alerting may suffice)
Real-time streaming is the requirement (use Kafka + Flink/Spark Streaming instead вҖ” DAG orchestrators are batch-oriented)
Real-World Example вҖ” ML Feature Pipeline
A production ML system needs fresh features every day. The pipeline fetches raw events, engineers features, validates them against expected statistics, and writes them to a feature store.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import pandas as pd

@dag(
    dag_id="ml_feature_pipeline",
    schedule_interval="0 3 * * *",          # 3 AM daily
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    default_args={
        "retries": 2,
        "retry_delay": timedelta(minutes=10),
        "execution_timeout": timedelta(hours=2),
    },
    catchup=False,
)
def ml_feature_pipeline():

    @task
    def fetch_user_events(execution_date=None) -> str:
        date_str = execution_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        path = f"s3://data-lake/events/{date_str}/events.parquet"
        # fetch_from_api(date_str).to_parquet(path)
        return path

    @task
    def fetch_transaction_data(execution_date=None) -> str:
        date_str = execution_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        path = f"s3://data-lake/transactions/{date_str}/txns.parquet"
        return path

    @task
    def engineer_user_features(events_path: str) -> str:
        df = pd.read_parquet(events_path)
        # feature engineering logic...
        out_path = events_path.replace("events", "user_features")
        df.to_parquet(out_path)
        return out_path

    @task
    def engineer_transaction_features(txn_path: str) -> str:
        df = pd.read_parquet(txn_path)
        out_path = txn_path.replace("transactions", "txn_features")
        df.to_parquet(out_path)
        return out_path

    @task
    def join_features(user_feat_path: str, txn_feat_path: str) -> str:
        user_df = pd.read_parquet(user_feat_path)
        txn_df = pd.read_parquet(txn_feat_path)
        combined = user_df.merge(txn_df, on="user_id")
        out_path = "s3://data-lake/features/combined.parquet"
        combined.to_parquet(out_path)
        return out_path

    @task
    def validate_features(features_path: str) -> str:
        df = pd.read_parquet(features_path)
        assert df.isnull().sum().sum() == 0, "Nulls detected in features"
        assert len(df) > 10_000, "Suspiciously few rows"
        return features_path

    @task
    def write_to_feature_store(features_path: str) -> None:
        df = pd.read_parquet(features_path)
        # write to Redis / Feast / Tecton...
        print(f"Wrote {len(df)} rows to feature store")

    # Dependency graph:
    # fetch_user_events в”Җв”Җв–ә engineer_user_features в”Җв”Җв–ә join_features в”Җв”Җв–ә validate в”Җв”Җв–ә write
    # fetch_transactions в”Җв”Җв–ә engineer_txn_features в”Җв”Җв”ҳ

    events_path = fetch_user_events()
    txn_path = fetch_transaction_data()

    # These two branches run in parallel
    user_feats = engineer_user_features(events_path)
    txn_feats = engineer_transaction_features(txn_path)

    # join waits for both branches
    combined = join_features(user_feats, txn_feats)
    validated = validate_features(combined)
    write_to_feature_store(validated)

dag = ml_feature_pipeline()
This DAG runs the two feature engineering branches in parallel (halving compute time), then joins and validates before writing to the feature store. If
fails, only that task retries вҖ” the transaction branch does not re-run.
1. Pushing large data through XCom / task return values
Storing DataFrames or large files in the orchestrator's metadata database causes database bloat and slow UI. Always write large data to S3/GCS/HDFS and pass only the path.
2. Importing heavy dependencies at DAG file parse time
Airflow parses every DAG file repeatedly (every 30 seconds by default). Heavy imports at the top level slow the scheduler significantly.

# BAD вҖ” imports a slow library at parse time
import tensorflow as tf

with DAG(...) as dag:
    ...

# GOOD вҖ” import inside the callable, only when the task actually runs
def train_model():
    import tensorflow as tf
    ...
Setting dependencies in multiple places without realising they form a cycle. The orchestrator will detect this and refuse to load the DAG, but it can be confusing to debug.
If a DAG has been paused for 6 months and
, Airflow will attempt to backfill every scheduled interval вҖ” potentially thousands of DAG runs at once. Always set
Tasks should use the execution context's logical date, not
, so backfilled runs process the correct time period.
6. Making tasks too fine-grained or too coarse

A task that does one database query is too fine (scheduling overhead dominates). A task that runs a 3-hour ETL is too coarse (a failure requires re-running 3 hours of work). A good task runs in 1вҖ“30 minutes and has a single clear responsibility.
, a hung task runs forever and blocks downstream work indefinitely.

Every task should produce the same result if run multiple times with the same inputs. This allows safe retries and backfills. Write data to a location keyed by the logical date so re-runs overwrite, not duplicate.
A task should either complete fully or leave no partial output. Write to a temporary location and move/rename on success (atomic rename in most file systems and object stores).
Each task does one thing: one extraction, one transformation, one load. This maximises parallelism and makes failures easy to isolate.
. The UI and logs display task IDs prominently.
slots to prevent too many tasks competing for the same external resource (database connection pool, API rate limit).
When a DAG has 30+ tasks, group related tasks visually and logically.

from airflow.utils.task_group import TaskGroup

with TaskGroup("feature_engineering") as feature_group:
    user_feats = engineer_user_features(events_path)
    txn_feats = engineer_transaction_features(txn_path)
Business logic belongs in importable Python modules, not inside DAG files. DAG files should only define the graph structure and wire up tasks.
Max total tasks running across all DAGs
Max concurrent tasks within one DAG run
Max concurrent runs of the same DAG
Resource group limits (e.g., max 5 DB tasks simultaneously)
Local, Celery, Kubernetes вҖ” determines where and how tasks run
вҖ” runs tasks as local processes on the scheduler machine; fine for development and small workloads
вҖ” distributes tasks to a pool of Celery workers; horizontally scalable; requires a message broker (Redis or RabbitMQ)
вҖ” spins up a fresh pod per task; perfect isolation and scaling; higher cold-start latency per task
вҖ” hybrid; short tasks use Celery, long/heavy tasks use Kubernetes pods

For pipelines that process many partitions (e.g., one task per country, per date, per customer segment), use dynamic task mapping rather than hardcoding fan-out. This allows the number of parallel branches to scale with data without changing DAG code.

For very large data volumes, keep orchestration tasks as thin coordinators: they submit Spark jobs, Dbt models, or SQL queries to dedicated compute engines rather than doing heavy computation inside the task process itself.
Most widely adopted; rich operator library; complex setup
Pythonic API; dynamic DAGs; excellent local dev experience
Simple; file-system target model; less active development
ML-focused; tight AWS integration; versioning built in
Pipeline framework + project structure; works with Airflow
SQL transformation DAGs; works within Airflow/Prefect
Kubernetes-native; YAML-defined; great for containerised tasks
Durable execution; long-running workflows; microservice focus
No retries, no parallelism, no visibility
No scheduling, no retry, no monitoring
No DAG structure; no scheduling built in
Heavy; not for orchestration logic; each job is one "task"
Dependency management, scheduling, retries, monitoring, UI
Infrastructure to run and maintain; learning curve
No dependency graph; no high-level orchestration
The pattern that emerges in mature data platforms is to use a DAG orchestrator (Airflow/Prefect/Dagster) as the
that coordinates execution, while delegating heavy computation to Spark, dbt, BigQuery, or Kubernetes jobs.

Never hardcode credentials in DAG files. Use the orchestrator's secret backend (Airflow's Connections, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Environment variables are acceptable but prefer a dedicated secrets backend for auditability and rotation.
appropriate to the upstream system's recovery time. Use
to prevent concurrent runs from corrupting shared state.
Ensure that retried tasks do not create duplicate records in databases. Use UPSERT/MERGE semantics, write to partition-keyed paths in object storage, or use a staging table with a final atomic swap.
Configure email or Slack alerts on

. Set up SLA miss detection to alert when a pipeline runs longer than expected. Use metrics exported from the orchestrator (Airflow exports Prometheus metrics) to track scheduler lag, task duration, and queue depth.

Use role-based access control (RBAC) in Airflow's UI to restrict who can trigger, pause, or modify production DAGs. Separate service accounts for each pipeline to limit blast radius of a compromised credential.
Treat DAG files as code вҖ” version them in Git, review changes through pull requests, and deploy through CI/CD. Never edit DAGs directly on the production scheduler machine.
A DAG is a dependency graph where nodes are tasks and directed edges declare "this must complete before that" вҖ” the orchestrator resolves execution order via topological sort
The acyclic constraint ensures the dependency graph has a valid resolution with no circular waits
Independent branches in a DAG run in parallel automatically, making proper DAG design a performance optimization, not just an organizational one
Tasks should be idempotent, atomic, and single-purpose so retries are safe and failures are cheap to recover from
Large data should flow through external storage (S3, GCS, databases); only small metadata passes between tasks through the orchestrator's XCom mechanism
Airflow excels in mature, large-scale production environments; Prefect offers a more Pythonic development experience; Dagster centers on data assets and lineage
вҖ” it should coordinate heavy compute, not perform it; push expensive work to Spark, dbt, or BigQuery
